title: Farrowing pen setup for an outdoor sow
subtitle: Shelter, fencing, and equipment for an outdoor farrowing.
excerpt: A sow farrowing outside needs a correctly prepared arc or hut, a heat lamp positioned for the creep area, adequate bedding depth, and secure perimeter fencing before she is moved in. Correct setup reduces piglet crushing and chilling in the first 48 hours, which are the two main causes of pre-weaning mortality.
sourceNotes: AHDB Pork, outdoor farrowing guide. DEFRA Welfare Code for Pigs (2003, updated guidance 2022). National Pig Association UK smallholder resources.

glossaryTerms.definition: A curved corrugated metal or timber shelter designed for outdoor sow farrowing. The arc shape sheds rain and resists tipping; most designs include a removable dividing board to restrict the sow to one end in the first days after farrowing.

A horizontal rail fixed to the inside walls of a farrowing pen at 20 to 25 cm from the floor. Creates a gap the sow cannot close against the wall, giving piglets space to escape when she lies down. Reduces crushing losses significantly.

A warm, enclosed corner of the farrowing pen accessible to piglets but blocked to the sow. The heat lamp is positioned above the creep. Piglets use the creep within hours of birth to rest away from the risk of the sow lying on them.

content.text: A sow should be moved into the prepared farrowing area five to seven days before her due date so she can settle into the space before farrowing begins. The preparation covers the
. Each element has a specific function; skipping any one of them increases piglet mortality.

Position the arc on well-drained ground with its entrance facing away from the prevailing wind. Bed it deeply with straw: a minimum of 20 cm at the back of the shelter. The sow will shape this into a nest. Anti-crush rails should be at 20 to 25 cm from the floor on both side walls. If the arc has no built-in rails, attach horizontal timber batons at this height before the sow enters. Piglets that feel the sow beginning to lie will retreat into the space behind the rail.

Hang the farrowing lamp over a corner of the pen the sow cannot reach: a section cordoned off at piglet height with a low board. The lamp should hang at 60 to 80 cm above the bedding; too close causes burns, too high fails to warm the area adequately. Check that the temperature at piglet level in the creep is approximately 30 to 32 degrees C in the first days. Piglets that pile together and shiver are too cold; piglets that spread away from the lamp are too warm.

The farrowing paddock should be electric-fenced with two strands: the lower strand at 20 cm to contain newborn piglets, the upper at 50 cm for the sow. Check all fence connections before moving the sow in. A sow that escapes a farrowing paddock with piglets presents a significant handling challenge and a risk to the piglets.

A lactating sow needs a minimum of 20 to 25 litres of water per day. Site the drinker at a convenient height for the sow but away from the bedding area to reduce wetting of the nest. Feed once or twice daily on a platform outside the shelter entrance, which encourages the sow to move and reduces time lying in the nest.
